target: crates/*/tests/ + crates/*/benches/ + bindings/napi/tests/ + packages/engine/src/*.test.ts + tools/create-benten-app/test/ at HEAD=dc396cf
scope_note: Post-implementation review after G1-G8 landed. Lens: vacuous-pass catches (tests named for behavior X that now pass for reason Y), stub-helper fidelity, pass-1 and pass-2 residual closure verification, new failure modes introduced during R5 fix-passes.

pass1_disposition_map.C2_toctou_reconciled: regressed — test BODIES reconciled to successful==100 at both sites, but the test FAILS at runtime because `schedule_revocation_at_iteration` returns NotImplemented Phase-2 and `iterate_write_handler` is an empty subgraph stub; compromise-regression surface is now vacuously red (see r4b-rtr-1)
pass1_disposition_map.C3_ivm_views_vacuous: partially closed — view1/view2/view5 populated-specific-CID tests now green; but the pass-2-flagged `rebuild_matches_incremental_state` trio now fails at runtime because `rebuild()` has no source-of-truth (pass-2 #5 materialized, see r4b-rtr-4)
pass1_disposition_map.M5_ivm_lag_two_sided: technically closed but semantically tautological — IVM publishes synchronously (benten-engine::change::publish is blocking), so lag is always 0 and `lag <= 1` + `observed > 0` are both trivially true per iteration (r4b-rtr-7)
pass1_disposition_map.M6_mvcc_snapshot_body: closed — body now opens snapshot, writes concurrently, asserts pre-write state visible, drops, asserts post-write (mvcc_snapshot.rs:33-71). Test is real.
pass1_disposition_map.M9_option_a_existence_leak: regressed — body asserts against SECURITY-POSTURE.md contents but the doc contains only Compromise #6; the `option A`/`E_CAP_DENIED_READ` anchors the test greps for are absent from the doc (r4b-rtr-2)
pass1_disposition_map.M13_durability_modes_bench: closed — both bench entry points now panic with `todo!(...)` sentinels so silent fall-through is impossible
pass1_disposition_map.M14_noauth_api_drift: closed — all three files canonically use NoAuthBackend::new() + WriteContext { label, is_privileged, actor_hint, ..default() }
pass1_disposition_map.M15_compromise_4_semantic: closed — Cargo.toml and CI YAML parsed semantically; regressed asserts against live strings
pass1_disposition_map.M16_proptests_landed: closed at count (14/14) but one duplicate is tautological — benten-core's `prop_subgraph_cid_order_independent` uses BTreeMap/BTreeSet min-merge internally so the 'order-independence' claim is proven by construction, not exercise (r4b-rtr-6). benten-eval's sibling test is real.
pass1_disposition_map.M23_soak_duration: closed — reduced to 500ms + 8 threads; CI_FULL_SOAK gate documented
pass1_disposition_map.M24_transform_grammar_offsets: closed — `.find(token)` computes offsets; hardcoded integers only at offset-0
pass1_disposition_map.p2_review_1_rebuild_matches_views_2_5: regressed — same as C3 above; pass-2 warned this would materialize, and it has
pass1_disposition_map.p2_review_2_blake3_grep_weak: open — compromise_6 still uses `posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128")`. With SECURITY-POSTURE.md now containing 'BLAKE3-256' and '128-bit' the assertion technically passes but the anchored-phrase fix was not applied
pass1_disposition_map.p2_review_3_evaluator_stack_dual_accept: open — `evaluator_stack.rs` still accepts both Ok(ON_CONFLICT) and Err(EvalError::WriteConflict) (r4b-rtr-5)
pass1_disposition_map.p2_qa_2_grant_backed_composition: CRITICAL — `.capability_policy_grant_backed()` and `.with_policy_allowing_revocation()` are both no-op placeholders returning `self` unchanged; `register_crud_with_grants` is a direct alias for `register_crud`. This makes 7+ cap-gate tests vacuously red or meaningless (r4b-rtr-3 — load-bearing)

findings.claim: Six `benten_engine::testing::*_handler` helper functions return `SubgraphSpec::empty(...)` with their `_max`/`_target` parameters prefixed-unused. The tests that depend on them cannot exercise the semantics they claim to test — writes-in-iteration, read-denials, per-primitive cap checks, attenuation escalation — because the subgraph has no primitives in it.
findings.detail: Affected helpers: `iterate_write_handler(_max)`, `minimal_write_handler()`, `read_handler_for(_target)`, `handler_declaring_read_but_writing_admin()`, `handler_with_call_attenuation_escalation()`, `handler_with_read_write_read_sequence()`. Each returns `SubgraphSpec::empty("<label>")`. Affected tests (currently red for stub reasons, not contract reasons): `capability_revoked_mid_iteration_denies_subsequent_batches`, `writes_in_current_batch_are_not_retroactively_denied`, `ucan_stub_error_routes_to_ON_ERROR_not_ON_DENIED`, `read_denied_returns_cap_denied_read`, `handler_cannot_escalate_via_call_attenuation`, `handler_with_understated_requires_denies_excess_writes`, `requires_checked_at_primitive_not_just_declaration`. When R5b fills these helpers the tests could start passing for reasons orthogonal to what they name (e.g. NoAuth allowing everything). This is the canonical R4b 'pass vacuously' failure mode.
findings.suggested_fix: Populate the six helpers with real SubgraphBuilder-constructed subgraphs matching their docstrings (iterate_write_handler: ITERATE+WRITE; read_handler_for: READ; handler_with_read_write_read_sequence: READ+WRITE+READ; etc.). Until that lands, mark these tests `#[ignore = "empty-subgraph stub; real handler deferred to G7+"]` so they don't silently pass on future R5b runs. Prefer populating over ignoring — the tests name real Phase-1 contracts.

findings.claim: Compromise #2 (E_CAP_DENIED_READ leaks existence, option A) is NOT documented in SECURITY-POSTURE.md. The doc contains only Compromise #6. The `option_a_existence_leak_is_documented_compromise` test greps for 'option A' or 'Option A' and 'E_CAP_DENIED_READ' in the doc and fails (as observed at HEAD). Named-compromise #2 is therefore unanchored in the written security posture.
findings.detail: SECURITY-POSTURE.md is 30 lines and covers only BLAKE3 128-bit. Named-compromise #2 (Ben-confirmed R1 triage) has no documentation anchor. The test this dispositioned at R4 triage M9 was specifically tightened to catch this, and it's doing its job — the doc just wasn't updated. If this had been green, a future flip to option B could silently happen without updating the posture doc.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `### Compromise #2 — E_CAP_DENIED_READ leaks existence (option A)` section to `docs/SECURITY-POSTURE.md` before R4b closes, naming both anchors the test greps for. One-paragraph explanation + the scope boundary (Phase 3 option-B revisit). This is a doc-slice commit; do it today.

findings.claim: Three load-bearing builder methods are no-op placeholders that lie about their scope. `capability_policy_grant_backed()` returns `self` unchanged, `with_policy_allowing_revocation()` returns `self` unchanged, `register_crud_with_grants(label)` is a direct alias for `register_crud(label)`. The whole cap-gate test suite (compromise_1/2, caps_crud, exit_3, cap_toctou) runs under NoAuthBackend silently because the builder method names suggest a policy swap that never happens.
findings.detail: Method bodies at lib.rs:1608 `#[must_use] pub fn capability_policy_grant_backed(self) -> Self { self }` and :1614 `#[must_use] pub fn with_policy_allowing_revocation(self) -> Self { self }`. Docstring at 1604 says 'Placeholder: the grant-backed capability policy. Phase 1 stub — the default NoAuth remains in force'. That makes every test like `caps_crud::revoked_cap_routes_to_on_denied` run against NoAuth, so the test CORRECTLY fails today (NoAuth allows everything, no ON_DENIED route) — but the failure mode is 'no policy switch happened' not 'real policy denied correctly'. Worse: if a future R5b move makes `register_crud_with_grants` route through a real grant-backed path and NoAuth policy stays, some of these tests might pass via different accidental routing. At minimum: the names are a lie that deceives reviewers.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) implement a Phase-1 minimal GrantBackedPolicy that reads `system:CapabilityGrant` Nodes from the backend and denies when absent/revoked (landing the missing half of Compromise #1 + #2 regressions), or (b) rename the builder methods to `_phase2_placeholder_grant_backed()` / `_phase2_placeholder_with_revocation()` and update the 5 consuming tests to be `#[ignore = "grant-backed policy is Phase 2"]`. Option (a) is preferred — it's what the named compromises require; option (b) is honest scoping. The current shape (misleading method names + silently-running-under-NoAuth) is neither.

findings.claim: `view1/2/5_rebuild_matches_incremental_state` and `prop_content_listing_incremental_equivalence` construct a fresh view, call `rebuild()` on it with NO source-of-truth (no backend, no event log argument), then compare the resulting empty state against a state built by `update(&event)`. For views where the test-query key was populated via the incremental path, this asserts `[specific_cid] == []` and fails. This was pass-2 finding #5; it has now materialized as red tests.
findings.detail: Observed: view1/view2/view5 rebuild tests fail with `left=[specific_cid], right=[]`; view3 proptest fails the same way. The `rebuild()` method is called on a view that has no backend handle and no event list — there is nothing to rebuild from. The test is architecturally incapable of passing: either `rebuild()` needs a `&dyn EventLog`/`&dyn KVBackend` parameter or the assertion needs to feed the rebuild path the same events the incremental path consumed. Pass-2 warned this would happen; R5 landed the implementation without touching the test design.
findings.suggested_fix: Modify `View::rebuild` signature to accept an event source (e.g. `fn rebuild(&mut self, events: &[ChangeEvent]) -> Result<(), ViewError>`) OR replace these four tests with a different rebuild-equivalence shape: apply N events twice to two separate view instances, assert equal state — the 'incremental vs incremental' symmetry. The current shape cannot distinguish a correct implementation from a broken one; delete it if `rebuild()` semantics aren't clearly pinned at the R4b cutoff.

findings.claim: Pass-2 finding #2 was dispositioned to R5 G6 for implementer to pick the shape. R5 G6 landed. The test still accepts both `Ok(r) if r.edge_label == "ON_CONFLICT"` AND `Err(EvalError::WriteConflict) => {}`. The sibling `primitive_read_write.rs::write_cas_wrong_version_routes_on_conflict` was tightened at R4 triage m3 to accept only the routed-edge shape. Two tests about the same WRITE CAS scenario disagree on the contract.
findings.detail: `evaluator_stack.rs` lines 69-73 retain the dual-accept match arm. Plan §2.5 E3 names typed error edges; the primitive_read_write test enforces that. This test silently allows the terminal-error shape, which means a future refactor could split one into routed + one into terminal without failing either test.
findings.suggested_fix: Tighten to `let r = ev.step(&op).expect("..."); assert_eq!(r.edge_label, "ON_CONFLICT");` matching the sibling. Drop the Err arm.

findings.claim: The benten-core-side proptest constructs `Node` via `build_node(ops)` which applies ops into a `BTreeSet<String>` for labels and a `BTreeMap<String, i64>` for properties (with a min-merge for duplicates). Since BTreeMap/BTreeSet are order-independent by construction, and the min-merge is order-independent by design, `build_node(ops) == build_node(reverse(ops))` holds before DAG-CBOR serialization. The proptest therefore proves 'BTreeMap is order-independent' rather than any property of Node::cid().
findings.detail: File comment explicitly says 'BTreeMap insertion-order independence combined with DAG-CBOR canonical sorting makes this test green' — confirming it's tautological by construction. Two Nodes that differ only in property-insertion order are already equal at the `Node` struct level; the `.cid()` call is incidental. The real test lives in benten-eval/tests/proptests_subgraph_order.rs (which exercises SubgraphBuilder). Test-name duplication across crates is also a minor papercut.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) delete the core-side copy (the docstring points to eval-side as the canonical test), or (b) rewrite it to exercise something core-owned that ISN'T trivially order-independent — e.g. a Node built by a hypothetical `add_label`/`add_property` mutator API, if and when one exists. Option (a) is correct.

findings.claim: IVM publish is synchronous (benten-engine::change::publish is a blocking for-loop over subscribers). For every iteration of the test, `observed == expected` holds — lag is always 0. The tightened `lag <= 1` + `observed > 0` assertions are both trivially true. The test name advertises a one-write lag bound contract that Phase 1 does not actually exercise because there's no async boundary.
findings.detail: change.rs:104-126 shows publish is a plain for-loop over Arc<Fn> callbacks with panic-catch. Ben's R4 pass-2 disposition p2-qa-4 acknowledged this: 'IVM lag `assert_eq!` tightening if barrier-sync is Phase 1 contract → R5 G5'. G5 landed, barriers are synchronous, test does not see >0 lag. Per-iteration the assertion is: 0 <= 1 (true) AND 0 > 0 (FALSE) — wait, observed=expected=i+1 so observed > 0 from first iteration. So both arms pass, but the test cannot catch a broken async-propagation path because there is no async path.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) tighten to `assert_eq!(observed, expected)` since Phase 1 promises synchronous publish — makes the contract precise and any future async drift fails loudly; or (b) add a comment `// Phase 1: publish is synchronous so lag is always 0; this test anchors the bound for when Phase 2 introduces async dispatch` and leave shape as-is. Recommend (a) — precise > aspirational.

findings.claim: Pass-2 finding #4 (BLAKE3-128 weak grep): the assertion `posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128")` now passes because SECURITY-POSTURE.md contains 'BLAKE3-256' and '128-bit effective collision resistance' literal strings. But the assertion doesn't check for the anchored phrase — a doc that said 'BLAKE3-256 supports 128 concurrent writers' would pass just as well. Pass-2 recommended the `"128-bit collision resistance"` anchored phrase; not applied.
findings.detail: Line 254-259 unchanged from pass-2. Compromise_4 got a semantic rewrite (YAML parsing); adjacent compromise_6 did not.
findings.suggested_fix: Tighten to `posture.contains("128-bit effective collision resistance")` (the exact phrase that SECURITY-POSTURE.md now uses at line 9) or `posture.contains("BLAKE3-256") && posture.contains("128-bit")` (both anchored).

findings.claim: The `requires_checked_at_primitive_not_just_declaration` test failure shows `Outcome::assert_success` panics with a verbose struct-Debug dump that leaks internal fields (`completed_iterations: None, successful_write_count: 0, error_code: Some("E_NOT_FOUND")`). When a test panics from inside `assert_success`, the diagnostic is misleading — the test wanted to check a counter delta, not to assert_success.
findings.detail: Helper-panic diagnostics are usually fine, but when the test chain is `.expect("call returns Ok wrapper").assert_success()` and the outcome routed via ON_NOT_FOUND (because the handler is empty), the test failure is 'assert_success panicked at lib.rs:2192' instead of 'handler_with_read_write_read_sequence is an empty stub'. This couples to finding r4b-rtr-1 — the root cause is the empty helper, but the test diagnostic makes it hard to see.
findings.suggested_fix: When r4b-rtr-1 is fixed (real handlers populated), this becomes moot. In the meantime, consider making `Outcome::assert_success` include the handler_id or the test name in the panic so failures say which handler is the stub.

C3 IVM view populated-specific-CID tests (view1_populated_read_returns_specific_cid_set et al): all green, asserting specific payload not just variant discriminant.

M13 durability bench: todo!() panics guard both entry points — silent fall-through impossible.

M14 NoAuthBackend canonical form: all three consumer files converged on NoAuthBackend::new() + WriteContext { label, is_privileged, actor_hint, .. } — the API drift the consolidator missed at R3 is now resolved.
M15 compromise_4 (WASM) semantic rewrite: Cargo.toml parsed for `wasmtime =`, wasm-checks.yml content-checked, workflow files scanned. Real structural verification.
M16 proptests landed 14/14 at count; benten-eval's prop_subgraph_cid_order_independent + prop_transform_deterministic are real exercise tests (note benten-core sibling in r4b-rtr-6).
M23 soak test 500ms + 8 threads + CI_FULL_SOAK gate: CI stability concern addressed.
M24 transform grammar offsets: `.find(token)` pattern is parser-shift-tolerant.
engine_crud.rs: 5 green tests covering update_node / delete_node / create_edge / edges_from — the CRUD surface gap identified at R4 M17 is filled with real assertions against real impl.
invariants_9_10_12.rs: 8 green tests including diagnostic-accessor coverage (determinism_class, cid_expected, cid_actual, violated_invariants) — the full quartet pattern R4 coverage asked for.
proptests_kvbackend_laws.rs: three green proptests (put-then-get, overwrite, delete-removes) running 33s with no regressions — real exercise of the KVBackend trait contract.
top_three_concerns: Six testing::* helpers return SubgraphSpec::empty(...) — 7 security-critical tests cannot exercise what their names claim. When R5b fills these helpers the tests could pass vacuously. This is the canonical R4b failure mode (r4b-rtr-1).

capability_policy_grant_backed() and register_crud_with_grants() are no-op placeholders — the whole cap-gate test suite runs silently under NoAuth, making Compromise #1/#2/#3 regression tests meaningless at HEAD. The builder method names are a lie (r4b-rtr-3).
docs/SECURITY-POSTURE.md is missing the Compromise #2 (option A) section that its own regression test greps for. Doc slice before R4b closes (r4b-rtr-2).

pass1_fix_verification.C2_toctou_reconciled: test shape fixed, runtime path vacuously red due to empty iterate_write_handler + schedule_revocation Phase-2 placeholder
pass1_fix_verification.C3_ivm_views_vacuous: partial — specific-CID tests fixed, but rebuild-matches-incremental fails architecturally as pass-2 predicted

notes: The pass-1 and pass-2 test-design fixes landed cleanly. What R4b exposes is a different failure mode: R5 populated placeholder IMPLEMENTATIONS (empty subgraph helpers, no-op builder hooks, Phase-2 NotImplemented returns) that make the tests structurally incapable of exercising their named contract. The 20 red tests divide roughly: 7 red for empty-stub-helper (r4b-rtr-1), 7 red for no-op builder hook (r4b-rtr-3), 4 red for architectural rebuild-design flaw (r4b-rtr-4), 2 red for genuine implementation gaps (change_event attribution + requires_property call-time check). The R5 group mini-reviewers correctly accepted these as Phase-2 scope; the problem is the TESTS name Phase-1 behavior, not Phase-2 behavior. Recommend: either re-scope the tests to match what Phase 1 actually ships (rename, #[ignore] with explanation), or land the minimal Phase-1 policy + populated helpers so the tests exercise real paths. The current state is the worst of both worlds — tests that LOOK like they enforce compromises but don't, against stubs that LOOK like they implement the compromises but don't.
